---
title: "Bayes in Action"
subtitle: "Worked examples you can check by hand, an interactive explorer, and the exercise set."
---

::: {.field-notes-band}
<div class="fn-eyebrow">Lesson 01 · Worked examples &amp; exercises</div>
<div class="fn-title">Bayes in Action</div>
<div class="fn-meta">
  <span class="fn-tag">Pencil-and-paper checkable</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">Interactive explorer</span>
  <span class="fn-tag">8 exercises + solutions</span>
</div>
:::

Every example on this page can be checked with pencil-and-paper arithmetic.
That is deliberate: if you can't count your way to the answer, you don't get to
trust the formula.

## Worked example 1 — The positive test result

**Setup.** A disease affects 1% of a screening population. The test has:

- **Sensitivity 95%** — if you're sick, it says positive 95% of the time.
- **Specificity 95%** — if you're healthy, it says negative 95% of the time
  (so 5% of healthy people get a false positive).

You test positive. How worried should you be?

**Count a population of 10,000 people:**

| | Test positive | Test negative | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sick (1%)** | 95 | 5 | 100 |
| **Healthy (99%)** | 495 | 9,405 | 9,900 |
| **Total** | **590** | 9,410 | 10,000 |

Everyone in your situation — a positive result — is one of the **590** people in
the first column. Of those, only **95** are actually sick:

$$P(\text{sick} \mid \text{positive}) = \frac{95}{590} \approx 16\%$$

A "95% accurate" test, and a positive result still means only a 16% chance of
disease. The false positives (495) outnumber the true positives (95) five to
one, because the healthy population is 99 times larger than the sick one.
**When the condition is rare, false positives dominate.** This is why screening
guidelines care so much about *who* gets screened: the same test applied to a
high-risk group (a higher prior) produces far more trustworthy positives.

## The interactive version {#the-interactive-version}

Reading that once is not the same as *feeling* it. Below, the three dials of the
problem are yours. Drag **prevalence** down toward a rare disease and watch the
false positives swamp the true ones; push **specificity** toward 100% and watch
trust in a positive result recover. The number that matters —
$P(\text{sick}\mid\text{positive})$ — updates live.

::: {.interactive-figure}
::: {.ojs-title}
Bayes posterior explorer — "I tested positive. Now what?"
:::

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-inputs
viewof prevalence = Inputs.range([0.001, 0.5], {value: 0.01, step: 0.001, label: "Prevalence (base rate)"})
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-inputs-2
viewof sensitivity = Inputs.range([0.50, 1.0], {value: 0.95, step: 0.005, label: "Sensitivity  P(+ | sick)"})
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-inputs-3
viewof specificity = Inputs.range([0.50, 1.0], {value: 0.95, step: 0.005, label: "Specificity  P(− | healthy)"})
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-compute
counts = {
  const N = 10000;
  const tp = prevalence * sensitivity * N;             // sick & positive
  const fn = prevalence * (1 - sensitivity) * N;        // sick & negative
  const fp = (1 - prevalence) * (1 - specificity) * N;  // healthy & positive
  const tn = (1 - prevalence) * specificity * N;        // healthy & negative
  const positives = tp + fp;
  const posterior = positives > 0 ? tp / positives : 0;
  return {N, tp, fn, fp, tn, positives, posterior};
}
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-readout
html`<div style="font-family: var(--bs-font-serif, serif); font-size: 1.15rem;
     line-height:1.4; margin: 0.2rem 0 1rem;">
  Out of <b>${counts.positives.toLocaleString(undefined,{maximumFractionDigits:0})}</b>
  people who test positive (per 10,000 screened),
  <b>${counts.tp.toLocaleString(undefined,{maximumFractionDigits:0})}</b> are truly sick.
  <br>
  <span style="font-size:1.9rem; color:${palette.oxide}; font-weight:700;">
    P(sick&nbsp;|&nbsp;positive) = ${(counts.posterior*100).toFixed(1)}%
  </span>
</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-bar
Plot.plot({
  height: 118,
  marginTop: 24,
  marginLeft: 8,
  marginRight: 8,
  x: {label: "People who test positive (per 10,000 screened) →", grid: false},
  y: {axis: null},
  color: {
    domain: ["Actually sick (true positive)", "Healthy (false positive)"],
    range: [palette.oxide, palette.slate],
    legend: true
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(
      [
        {group: "pos", who: "Actually sick (true positive)", n: counts.tp},
        {group: "pos", who: "Healthy (false positive)", n: counts.fp}
      ],
      {y: "group", x: "n", fill: "who", order: ["Actually sick (true positive)"]}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-curve
Plot.plot({
  height: 240,
  marginLeft: 52,
  x: {label: "Prevalence (base rate) →", tickFormat: "%", grid: true},
  y: {label: "↑ P(sick | positive)", tickFormat: "%", domain: [0, 1], grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.line(
      d3.range(0.001, 0.5, 0.002).map(p => ({
        p,
        post: (p*sensitivity) / (p*sensitivity + (1-p)*(1-specificity))
      })),
      {x: "p", y: "post", stroke: palette.slate, strokeWidth: 2.5}
    ),
    Plot.dot([{p: prevalence, post: counts.posterior}],
      {x: "p", y: "post", r: 6, fill: palette.oxide, stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.text([{p: prevalence, post: counts.posterior}], {
      x: "p", y: "post", text: d => `${(d.post*100).toFixed(0)}%`,
      dy: -14, fontWeight: 700, fill: palette.oxide
    })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| label: bayes-palette
//| echo: false
// Match the figure to the site's field-notebook palette.
palette = ({oxide: "#b0472e", slate: "#3d5a6c", moss: "#5a6f3f", ochre: "#c98a26"})
```

*The curve is the whole lesson in one line: hold the test fixed and simply vary
how common the disease is. A test that is trustworthy for a common condition
becomes nearly useless for a rare one — not because the test changed, but
because the prior did.*
:::

::: {.lens}
An interviewer's follow-up to this figure is almost always the same: *"Your
classifier has 95% sensitivity and 95% specificity and you're deploying it to
flag a condition that affects 1 in 500 samples. What precision should you
expect, and what will you do about it?"* This figure **is** that question —
precision is exactly $P(\text{sick}\mid\text{positive})$. Answer: dismal
precision, and you either raise the threshold, enrich the screened population,
or add a confirmatory second test. That last option is the next section.
:::

### The second test — updating again

Yesterday's posterior is today's prior. Take the 590 positives and test them
again (assume the second test errs independently):

- Of the 95 sick: 95% test positive again → **90.25**
- Of the 495 healthy: 5% test positive again → **24.75**

$$P(\text{sick} \mid \text{two positives}) = \frac{90.25}{90.25 + 24.75} \approx 78\%$$

Two cheap, imperfect tests took you from 1% → 16% → 78%. Evidence compounds.

### The same journey in odds form

The odds form makes the compounding visible. A positive result multiplies your
odds by the **likelihood ratio**
$LR^+ = \frac{\text{sensitivity}}{1 - \text{specificity}} = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19$:

$$\underbrace{\frac{1}{99}}_{\text{prior odds}} \times 19 \times 19 = \frac{361}{99} \approx 3.65 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{3.65}{1+3.65} \approx 78\%$$

One multiplication per piece of evidence. This is the form working
diagnosticians and forensic analysts actually use.

## Worked example 2 — A spam filter, by hand

A naive Bayes spam filter is Worked Example 1 wearing a different shirt: each
word in an email is a "test result," and the filter multiplies one likelihood
ratio per word.

**Setup.** Suppose 50% of incoming mail is spam (prior odds 1:1), and from
historical mail we know:

| Word | Appears in spam | Appears in legit mail | Likelihood ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| "winner" | 20% | 1% | 20 |
| "free" | 30% | 5% | 6 |
| "meeting" | 2% | 20% | 0.1 |

**An email arrives containing "winner" and "free":**

$$\text{posterior odds} = 1 \times 20 \times 6 = 120 \;\Rightarrow\; P(\text{spam}) = \frac{120}{121} \approx 99.2\%$$

**Now suppose it also contains "meeting":**

$$120 \times 0.1 = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; P(\text{spam}) = \frac{12}{13} \approx 92\%$$

Evidence can push in both directions, and no single word decides anything — the
verdict is the *product* of many small nudges. The "naive" in naive Bayes is the
assumption that words appear independently given spam/not-spam. It's false
("free" and "winner" travel together), which makes the filter overconfident —
but the *ranking* of spam vs. not-spam survives, which is why this 1990s-era
method still ships in production systems today.

::: {.specimen}
**One pattern, many costumes**

Medical retesting and spam filtering are the same computation: **prior odds ×
a likelihood ratio per piece of evidence.** Election forecasting is the same
computation with polls as evidence. The Air France 447 search [@stone2014] was
the same computation over a map grid — each searched-and-empty cell multiplied
that cell's odds of containing the wreck by a likelihood ratio *below 1*.
Learn the pattern once and you own all of them.
:::

## Exercise set

Work each problem by counting first; use the formula only to check. Solutions
are collapsed — genuinely attempt each one before looking. For conceptual,
interview-style questions, continue to the [Interview Problems](interview.qmd)
page.

### 1. The breathalyzer

One in 1,000 drivers on the road tonight is over the legal limit. A checkpoint
breathalyzer catches 100% of drunk drivers but also reads positive for 5% of
sober drivers. A driver blows positive. What's the probability they're actually
over the limit?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
Count 1,000 drivers: 1 drunk (tests positive) and 999 sober, of whom about 50
(5%) falsely test positive. Of ~51 positives, 1 is drunk: $P \approx 1/51
\approx 2\%$. Even a test that is *perfect at catching* drunk drivers is swamped
by false positives at a 0.1% base rate. (Set the explorer above to prevalence
0.001, sensitivity 1.0, specificity 0.95 and read it off the bar.)
:::

### 2. Mammography (the classic physician-survey problem)

1% of women in a screening cohort have breast cancer. Mammography detects 90% of
cancers and gives false positives 9% of the time. A woman screens positive —
what's the probability she has cancer? (When Gigerenzer posed this to physicians,
typical answers were 70–90% [@gigerenzer2007].)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
Per 10,000: 100 with cancer → 90 true positives; 9,900 without → 891 false
positives. $P = 90/981 \approx 9\%$. The physicians' typical answer was off by
roughly a factor of ten.
:::

### 3. The taxi cab problem (Kahneman & Tversky)

85% of a city's cabs are Green, 15% are Blue. A hit-and-run witness says the cab
was Blue, and testing shows the witness correctly identifies cab color 80% of
the time. What's the probability the cab was actually Blue?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
Per 100 cabs: 15 Blue → witness says "Blue" for 12; 85 Green → witness wrongly
says "Blue" for 17. $P = 12/29 \approx 41\%$. Despite a fairly reliable witness,
the cab is *more likely Green* — the base rate is doing that work [@tversky1974].
:::

### 4. Chaining evidence in odds form

Take the disease from Worked Example 1 (prior 1%, $LR^+ = 19$). The test also
has a *negative* likelihood ratio
$LR^- = \frac{1-\text{sensitivity}}{\text{specificity}} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} \approx 0.053$.
A patient tests positive, then negative on the independent retest. Where does
their probability land?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
$\frac{1}{99} \times 19 \times 0.053 \approx 0.0102$ odds → $P \approx 1\%$. The
two results essentially cancel ($19 \times 0.053 \approx 1$), returning the
patient almost exactly to the prior. Conflicting evidence doesn't average — it
multiplies.
:::

### 5. Build the filter

Using the word table from Worked Example 2, classify an email containing "free"
and "meeting" (and neither of the other words). Start from 1:1 prior odds. Spam
or not?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
$1 \times 6 \times 0.1 = 0.6$ odds → $P(\text{spam}) = 0.6/1.6 = 37.5\%$. Leans
legitimate. A single business-y word outweighed "free."
:::

### 6. The prosecutor's fallacy

A DNA sample from a crime scene matches a defendant. An expert testifies the
probability of a random person matching is 1 in a million, and the prosecutor
argues this means a 999,999-in-a-million probability of guilt. The crime
occurred in a metro area of 10 million people. What's wrong, and what does a
Bayesian analysis say?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
The expert stated $P(\text{match} \mid \text{innocent})$; the prosecutor sold it
as $P(\text{innocent} \mid \text{match})$. In a population of 10 million, about
10 innocent people match by chance, so *absent other evidence* the defendant is
one of ~11 matching people: $P(\text{guilty} \mid \text{match}) \approx 9\%$,
not 99.9999%. Other evidence (opportunity, motive) enters as additional
likelihood ratios — which is exactly how it should be argued.
:::

### 7. When the forecast "fails"

A forecaster gave a candidate a 29% chance of winning; the candidate won. A
pundit declares the model "catastrophically wrong." Write two or three sentences
evaluating the pundit's claim, using the concept of calibration.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution
One outcome cannot falsify a 29% forecast — events with 29% probability should
happen about 29% of the time, roughly two of every seven such forecasts. The
model is judged across *many* forecasts: if its "29%" events happen far more or
less often than 29%, *that* is evidence of miscalibration. (A useful check on
the pundit: would they have credited the model as "catastrophically right" had
the 71% side won?)
:::

### 8. Find one in the wild (mini-project)

Find a news article, press release, or paper abstract that reports a test,
detector, or risk factor's accuracy without the base rate — or that flips a
conditional probability. Write a one-page Bayesian correction: identify the
prior, the likelihood ratio, and compute the posterior under a plausible base
rate. State how sensitive your conclusion is to the prior you chose.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Grading guide
Full credit requires: (a) correct identification of which conditional the source
reported, (b) an explicit, defended base rate, (c) a correct posterior, and
(d) a sensitivity statement ("under priors from X to Y, the posterior ranges
from…"). The sensitivity statement is the part most learners skip and the part
that most resembles real statistical practice.
:::

::: {.lesson-nav}
<span>[← Background Primer](background.qmd)</span>
<span>[Interview Problems →](interview.qmd)</span>
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
